---
title: "Why Correlations Can't Tell Us Whether Muscle Size Causes Strength"
subtitle: "Part I of III — Associations and Their Discontents"
date: "2026-06-13"
categories: [causal inference, muscle physiology, statistics, simulation]
description: >
  Correlations between changes in muscle size and strength vary wildly across
  studies — anywhere from near zero to above 0.9. But this variability tells
  us less about biology than we might think: the same training programme, run
  twice, can produce almost any correlation depending on who you recruit, how
  you measure change, and which causal forces happen to dominate in your sample.
  This article uses interactive simulations to show the three structural reasons
  associations arise — and why none of them, on their own, can tell us whether
  size actually causes strength.
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show R code"
filters:
  - shinylive
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Key points

- Correlations between changes in muscle size and strength vary from near zero 
  to above 0.9 across studies — and that variability is itself the problem, 
  not the answer.
- A high correlation can emerge even when size has no causal effect on strength 
  whatsoever, through at least three distinct structural mechanisms.
- Even when the causal effect is real, the correlation you observe depends 
  heavily on choices made before the analysis — who you recruit, how you measure 
  change, and what scale you report it on.
- Establishing that size *causes* strength requires a different framework 
  entirely, which we introduce in Part II.
:::

You have probably heard that correlation is not causation. It is one of those phrases that gets repeated so often it has almost lost its meaning — a disclaimer people attach to findings before moving on to interpret them causally anyway. But if everyone knows this, why do we keep reaching for correlations when we want to understand whether muscle growth drives strength gains?

I think the honest answer is that we are trying to answer a causal question and do not always know how to do it properly. Correlations are easy to compute, easy to report, and — crucially — easy to misread as evidence that one thing is causing another. The literature on muscle size and strength is a good example: observed correlations between changes in the two vary anywhere from near zero to above 0.9 across studies, and that variability itself is rarely treated as the puzzle it should be.

The deeper problem is not which correlation we pick, or how cleverly we estimate it. It is that no correlation, however well-constructed, can answer the question we actually care about: does gaining muscle make you stronger?

That question is causal. Correlations are not. This is not a philosophical distinction — it has real consequences for how we interpret training research and design interventions. In this article, we use interactive simulations to make those consequences concrete. We first show how a strong association between size and strength changes can emerge even when one has nothing to do with the other. We then show how, even in a world where size genuinely does cause strength, the number we report can change dramatically depending on choices that have nothing to do with the underlying biology.

---

## Part I — Three Ways Associations Arise

Association: two variables are associated if information about one tells you something about the likelihood of the other (statistical correlation)

Every systematic association between muscle size ($M$) and strength ($S$) traces back to one of three causal structures — or, in small samples, to chance alone. We can represent these structures with **directed acyclic graphs (DAGs)**, where an arrow from $A$ to $B$ means "$A$ is a cause of $B$." No arrow means no direct causal relationship. What makes DAGs useful is that they let us read off, just from the diagram, which variables we would expect to be associated — and why. Below we walk through each scenario in turn.

### Structure 1 — Direct Causation

```{=html}
<div class="dag-figure">
<svg viewBox="0 0 360 120" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <defs>
    <marker id="d1a" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="7" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0,8 3,0 6" class="dag-arr-fill"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <rect x="20" y="34" width="148" height="52" rx="6" class="dag-node"/>
  <text x="94" y="54" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-cat">MUSCLE SIZE</text>
  <text x="94" y="74" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-var">M</text>
  <rect x="192" y="34" width="148" height="52" rx="6" class="dag-node"/>
  <text x="266" y="54" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-cat">STRENGTH</text>
  <text x="266" y="74" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-var">S</text>
  <line x1="169" y1="60" x2="190" y2="60" class="dag-arrow" marker-end="url(#d1a)"/>
</svg>
<div class="dag-caption">M = Muscle Size · S = Strength</div>
</div>
```

In this scenario, muscle growth directly causes strength to increase. This is what we would generally expect: when one variable has a causal effect on another, we should also expect the two to be associated. A correlation between $\Delta M$ and $\Delta S$ is therefore telling you something real — the two are linked because one drives the other.

A helpful way to think about DAGs is to imagine the arrows as pipes through which association can flow. If two variables are connected by a pipe, they will tend to move together. In this scenario, M and S are connected by a single direct pipe — so when M goes up, S tends to go up too. The trouble, as we will see, is that pipes can carry association between variables even when neither causes the other.

### Structure 2 — Common Cause (Confounding)

```{=html}
<div class="dag-figure">
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 238" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <defs>
    <marker id="d2a" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="7" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0,8 3,0 6" class="dag-arr-fill"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <rect x="255" y="20" width="150" height="52" rx="6" class="dag-node"/>
  <text x="330" y="40" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-cat">TRAINING</text>
  <text x="330" y="59" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-var">T</text>
  <rect x="50" y="158" width="148" height="52" rx="6" class="dag-node"/>
  <text x="124" y="177" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-cat">MUSCLE SIZE</text>
  <text x="124" y="196" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-var">M</text>
  <rect x="462" y="158" width="148" height="52" rx="6" class="dag-node"/>
  <text x="536" y="177" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-cat">STRENGTH</text>
  <text x="536" y="196" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-var">S</text>
  <line x1="292" y1="72" x2="170" y2="156" class="dag-arrow" marker-end="url(#d2a)"/>
  <line x1="368" y1="72" x2="490" y2="156" class="dag-arrow" marker-end="url(#d2a)"/>
  <path d="M198,184 Q330,214 462,184" class="dag-spurious"/>
  <text x="330" y="226" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-note">SPURIOUS ASSOCIATION</text>
</svg>
<div class="dag-caption">T drives both M and S independently — their correlation carries no causal meaning</div>
</div>
```

In this scenario, neither M nor S causes the other. Instead, both are driven by a common cause — training. Because both variables share the same upstream cause, they will tend to move together in any dataset, even without a direct connection between them.

This means that if you learn someone gained a lot of muscle over a training block, you would reasonably update your expectation of their strength gains upward — not because muscle caused the strength gain, but because both are downstream of the same training stimulus. A correlation between $\Delta M$ and $\Delta S$ will appear, and it may be substantial, even when the direct effect of M on S is exactly zero.

### Structure 3 — Collider Bias (Selection)

```{=html}
<div class="dag-figure">
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 185" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <defs>
    <marker id="d3a" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="7" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0,8 3,0 6" class="dag-arr-fill"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="d3b" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="7" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0,8 3,0 6" class="dag-spu-fill"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <rect x="20" y="46" width="148" height="52" rx="6" class="dag-node"/>
  <text x="94" y="66" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-cat">MUSCLE SIZE</text>
  <text x="94" y="85" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-var">M</text>
  <rect x="248" y="40" width="164" height="64" rx="9" class="dag-node-ring"/>
  <text x="330" y="34" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-cond">CONDITIONED ON</text>
  <rect x="256" y="46" width="148" height="52" rx="6" class="dag-node-alt"/>
  <text x="330" y="64" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-cat">PROGRESS SCORE</text>
  <text x="330" y="85" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-var">P</text>
  <rect x="492" y="46" width="148" height="52" rx="6" class="dag-node"/>
  <text x="566" y="66" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-cat">STRENGTH</text>
  <text x="566" y="85" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-var">S</text>
  <line x1="169" y1="72" x2="254" y2="72" class="dag-arrow" marker-end="url(#d3a)"/>
  <line x1="492" y1="72" x2="406" y2="72" class="dag-arrow" marker-end="url(#d3a)"/>
  <path d="M94,98 C94,150 566,150 566,98" class="dag-spurious" marker-end="url(#d3b)"/>
  <text x="330" y="165" text-anchor="middle" class="dag-lbl-note">SPURIOUS PATH · OPENS WHEN CONDITIONING ON P</text>
</svg>
<div class="dag-caption">P is a collider — both M and S cause P, so conditioning on P opens a spurious path between them</div>
</div>
```

### Scenario 3 — Collider Bias (Selection)

In this scenario, M and S are again causally unrelated — and unlike Scenario 2, they do not even share a common cause. Both independently influence a third variable: whether a participant completes the study. Someone who gained little muscle might still complete the programme because their strength progressed well enough to keep them motivated, and vice versa. Study completion is therefore a common *effect* of both M and S — a collider.

Here is the key insight: in the full population, M and S are independent. Learning that someone gained a lot of muscle tells you nothing about their strength gains, because there is no pipe connecting them — the only path runs through the collider, and colliders, unlike common causes, *block* the flow of association rather than transmitting it.

But the moment we restrict our analysis to completers — conditioning on the collider — that blockage disappears. Within completers, those who gained little muscle must have gained more strength to make the cut, and those who gained little strength must have gained more muscle. A spurious association between M and S appears entirely as an artefact of who ended up in the analysed sample, not because of anything happening biologically between the two variables.

---

### 🔬 Simulation 1 — Which World Are We In?

The simulation below generates data from three different causal worlds. Rather 
than letting you dial in arbitrary values, we have pre-set the parameters to 
reflect realistic scenarios grounded in the resistance training literature. 
Click a scenario to load it, then hit **Resample** to build intuition across 
repeated draws from the same world.

```{shinylive-r}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 620

library(shiny)
library(ggplot2)

# ── Dark theme for ggplot ──────────────────────────────────────
theme_dark_custom <- function() {
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(
      plot.background    = element_rect(fill = "#1e1e1e", colour = NA),
      panel.background   = element_rect(fill = "#1e1e1e", colour = NA),
      panel.grid.major   = element_line(colour = "#333333"),
      panel.grid.minor   = element_line(colour = "#2a2a2a"),
      axis.text          = element_text(colour = "#aaaaaa"),
      axis.title         = element_text(colour = "#cccccc"),
      plot.title         = element_text(colour = "#ffffff", face = "bold",
                                        size = 14),
      plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "#aaaaaa", size = 11),
      legend.background  = element_rect(fill = "#1e1e1e", colour = NA),
      legend.text        = element_text(colour = "#aaaaaa"),
      legend.title       = element_text(colour = "#cccccc")
    )
}

# ── Presets ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
presets <- list(
  direct = list(
    b_ts = 0.60, b_sf = 0.55, b_tf = 0.30, sig = 0.35,
    label = "Scenario 1 — Direct causation",
    subtitle = "Size → Strength effect is active. Training also drives strength directly, but size plays a meaningful causal role."
  ),
  confound = list(
    b_ts = 0.30, b_sf = 0.00, b_tf = 0.90, sig = 0.40,
    label = "Scenario 2 — Confounding",
    subtitle = "Size → Strength is zero. Training drives both independently. Based on typical effect sizes in resistance training studies."
  ),
  collider = list(
    b_ts = NA, b_sf = NA, b_tf = NA, sig = NA,
    label = "Scenario 3 — Collider (Powerlifting selection)",
    subtitle = "M and S are independent. Only athletes strong AND muscular enough to compete appear in the dataset."
  )
)

ui <- fluidPage(
  tags$head(tags$style(HTML("
    body, .container-fluid {
      background-color: #1e1e1e !important;
      color: #cccccc !important;
      font-family: 'Helvetica Neue', sans-serif;
      font-size: 13px;
    }
    .well {
      background-color: #2a2a2a !important;
      border: 1px solid #3a3a3a !important;
      color: #cccccc !important;
    }
    h4 {
      font-size: 12px;
      font-weight: 700;
      text-transform: uppercase;
      letter-spacing: 0.1em;
      color: #5b9ec9;
      margin-bottom: 10px;
    }
    .stat-label {
      font-size: 11px;
      color: #777;
      text-transform: uppercase;
      letter-spacing: 0.1em;
      margin-top: 10px;
    }
    .stat-value {
      font-size: 1.5rem;
      font-weight: 700;
      color: #5b9ec9;
    }
    .stat-value.green { color: #4caf82; }
    .stat-value.red   { color: #e05c5c; }
    .preset-btn {
      display: block;
      width: 100%;
      margin-bottom: 8px;
      text-align: left;
      background-color: #2f2f2f;
      border: 1px solid #444;
      color: #cccccc;
      padding: 8px 12px;
      border-radius: 3px;
      cursor: pointer;
      font-size: 12px;
      font-weight: 600;
    }
    .preset-btn:hover  { background-color: #3a3a3a; border-color: #5b9ec9; }
    .preset-btn.active { background-color: #1a3a52; border-color: #5b9ec9;
                         color: #ffffff; }
    .scenario-subtitle {
      font-size: 11px;
      color: #888;
      line-height: 1.5;
      margin-bottom: 12px;
      font-style: italic;
    }
    hr { border-color: #333; }
    label { color: #aaaaaa !important; }
    .irs--shiny .irs-bar      { background: #5b9ec9; border-color: #5b9ec9; }
    .irs--shiny .irs-handle   { border-color: #5b9ec9; }
    .irs--shiny .irs-from,
    .irs--shiny .irs-to,
    .irs--shiny .irs-single   { background: #5b9ec9; }
    .irs--shiny .irs-line     { background: #444; border-color: #444; }
    .irs--shiny .irs-grid-text { color: #666; }
    .btn-primary {
      background-color: #5b9ec9 !important;
      border-color: #5b9ec9 !important;
      color: #fff !important;
      font-size: 12px;
      font-weight: 700;
      letter-spacing: 0.05em;
    }
    .btn-primary:hover { background-color: #4a8ab5 !important; }
    .note-text {
      font-size: 11px;
      color: #666;
      margin-top: 8px;
    }
  "))),

  fluidRow(
    # ── Left panel ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    column(4,
      wellPanel(
        h4("Choose a scenario"),
        tags$button("Scenario 1 — Direct causation",
                    id = "btn_direct",
                    class = "preset-btn active",
                    onclick = "Shiny.setInputValue('preset', 'direct',
                               {priority: 'event'})"),
        tags$button("Scenario 2 — Confounding",
                    id = "btn_confound",
                    class = "preset-btn",
                    onclick = "Shiny.setInputValue('preset', 'confound',
                               {priority: 'event'})"),
        tags$button("Scenario 3 — Collider / Selection",
                    id = "btn_collider",
                    class = "preset-btn",
                    onclick = "Shiny.setInputValue('preset', 'collider',
                               {priority: 'event'})"),
        hr(),
        h4("Parameters"),
        uiOutput("scenario_subtitle"),
        sliderInput("b_ts", "Training → Size",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.60, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("b_sf", "Size → Strength",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.55, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("b_tf", "Training → Strength (direct)",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.30, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("sig",  "Noise",
                    min=0.05, max=1, value=0.35, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("n",    "N (participants)",
                    min=50, max=600, value=200, step=50),
        actionButton("resample", "Resample",
                     class = "btn btn-primary btn-sm")
      )
    ),

    # ── Right panel ─────────────────────────────────────────────
    column(8,
      plotOutput("scatter", height = "340px"),
      fluidRow(
        column(4,
          div(class="stat-label", "Observed r"),
          div(class="stat-value", textOutput("r_obs", inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(4,
          div(class="stat-label", "Causal share of variance"),
          uiOutput("causal_share")
        ),
        column(4,
          div(class="stat-label", "Active scenario"),
          div(class="stat-value", style="font-size:1rem",
              textOutput("world_label", inline=TRUE))
        )
      ),
      div(class="note-text",
        "Collider world: grey points = full population (M and S independent); ",
        "red points = athletes who made it into the dataset. ",
        "Regression line fitted to red points only."
      )
    )
  ),

  # ── Button highlight JS ──────────────────────────────────────
  tags$script(HTML("
    Shiny.addCustomMessageHandler('highlight_btn', function(id) {
      ['btn_direct','btn_confound','btn_collider'].forEach(function(b) {
        document.getElementById(b).classList.remove('active');
      });
      document.getElementById(id).classList.add('active');
    });
  "))
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {

  current_world <- reactiveVal("direct")

  # Load preset when button clicked
  observeEvent(input$preset, {
    p <- presets[[input$preset]]
    current_world(input$preset)
    session$sendCustomMessage("highlight_btn", paste0("btn_", input$preset))

    if (input$preset != "collider") {
      updateSliderInput(session, "b_ts", value = p$b_ts)
      updateSliderInput(session, "b_sf", value = p$b_sf)
      updateSliderInput(session, "b_tf", value = p$b_tf)
      updateSliderInput(session, "sig",  value = p$sig)
    }
  })

  output$scenario_subtitle <- renderUI({
    p <- presets[[current_world()]]
    div(class="scenario-subtitle", p$subtitle)
  })

  # ── Data generation ──────────────────────────────────────────
  dat <- reactive({
    input$resample
    input$preset
    n    <- input$n
    b_ts <- input$b_ts
    b_sf <- input$b_sf
    b_tf <- input$b_tf
    sig  <- input$sig
    world <- current_world()

    if (world == "direct") {
      training <- rnorm(n)
      dM <- b_ts * training + sig * rnorm(n)
      dS <- b_sf * dM + b_tf * training + sig * rnorm(n)
      selected <- rep(TRUE, n)

    } else if (world == "confound") {
      training <- rnorm(n)
      dM <- b_ts * training + sig * rnorm(n)
      dS <- b_tf * training + sig * rnorm(n)   # M → S path is zero
      selected <- rep(TRUE, n)

    } else {
      # Collider: powerlifting selection
      # M and S are generated independently
      # Both contribute to "making it" as a competitive powerlifter
      dM <- rnorm(n)
      dS <- rnorm(n)
      # Selection score: high muscle AND high strength → compete
      logit_select <- 1.2 * dM + 1.2 * dS - 0.5 + 0.4 * rnorm(n)
      prob_select  <- 1 / (1 + exp(-logit_select))
      selected     <- runif(n) < prob_select
    }

    data.frame(dM=dM, dS=dS, selected=selected)
  })

  # ── Plot ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  output$scatter <- renderPlot({
    d     <- dat()
    world <- current_world()

    if (world == "collider") {
      d_sel   <- d[d$selected,  ]
      d_unsel <- d[!d$selected, ]
      r_val   <- if (nrow(d_sel) > 2) cor(d_sel$dM, d_sel$dS) else NA

      ggplot() +
        geom_point(data=d_unsel, aes(x=dM, y=dS),
                   colour="#555555", alpha=0.25, size=1.5) +
        geom_point(data=d_sel, aes(x=dM, y=dS),
                   colour="#e05c5c", alpha=0.55, size=2) +
        geom_smooth(data=d_sel, aes(x=dM, y=dS),
                    method="lm", se=TRUE,
                    colour="#e05c5c", fill="#e05c5c",
                    alpha=0.15, linetype="dashed", linewidth=0.9) +
        labs(
          x = "Muscle size (M)",
          y = "Strength (S)",
          title = sprintf("Observed r = %.2f  (selected only)",
                          ifelse(is.na(r_val), NA, r_val)),
          subtitle = sprintf("N total = %d  |  N selected (competing) = %d  |  N excluded = %d",
                             nrow(d), sum(d$selected), sum(!d$selected))
        ) +
        theme_dark_custom()

    } else {
      r_val <- cor(d$dM, d$dS)
      ggplot(d, aes(x=dM, y=dS)) +
        geom_point(colour="#5b9ec9", alpha=0.40, size=1.8) +
        geom_smooth(method="lm", se=TRUE,
                    colour="#e05c5c", fill="#e05c5c",
                    alpha=0.15, linetype="dashed", linewidth=0.9) +
        labs(
          x = "Δ Muscle size (M)",
          y = "Δ Strength (S)",
          title = sprintf("Observed r = %.2f", r_val),
          subtitle = sprintf("N = %d", nrow(d))
        ) +
        theme_dark_custom()
    }
  }, bg="#1e1e1e")

  # ── Stats ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  output$r_obs <- renderText({
    d     <- dat()
    world <- current_world()
    d_plot <- if (world == "collider") d[d$selected, ] else d
    if (nrow(d_plot) < 3) return("—")
    sprintf("%.2f", cor(d_plot$dM, d_plot$dS))
  })

  output$causal_share <- renderUI({
    world <- current_world()
    if (world == "direct") {
      total <- input$b_sf^2 + input$b_tf^2 + input$sig^2 + 1e-4
      pct   <- round(100 * input$b_sf^2 / total)
      div(class="stat-value green", paste0(pct, "%"))
    } else if (world == "confound") {
      div(class="stat-value red", "0%")
    } else {
      div(class="stat-value", style="color:#888;font-size:1rem",
          "0% — M and S are independent")
    }
  })

  output$world_label <- renderText({
    switch(current_world(),
      direct   = "Direct causation",
      confound = "Confounding",
      collider = "Collider / Selection"
    )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## What to look for

- **Scenario 1:** The causal share of variance tells you what fraction of the 
  observed correlation is genuinely due to M causing S. Even here, the 
  correlation overstates the isolated causal effect because training drives 
  both simultaneously.
- **Scenario 2:** Set *Size → Strength* is already at zero — yet watch a 
  meaningful correlation appear, driven entirely by training as a shared cause. 
  This mirrors the logic behind Ahtiainen et al. (2016).
- **Scenario 3:** Grey points are the full population — M and S are completely 
  independent. Red points are athletes who made it into a competitive powerlifting 
  dataset. The regression line is fitted to red points only. A spurious 
  association emerges purely from who got selected.
:::

---

## Part II — The Scale Problem: Additive vs. Multiplicative Worlds

Suppose we grant, for the sake of argument, that muscle size truly causes
strength — Structure 1 is the true DAG. We would still face a second, independent
problem: **we do not know the functional form of the biological relationship.**

Consider two hypothetical worlds:

**World A — Additive:** Each additional cm³ of muscle adds the same absolute
number of Newtons, regardless of starting size. The relationship between ΔSize
and ΔStrength is linear on absolute scales.

**World B — Multiplicative:** Each additional cm³ of muscle adds the same
*percentage* of current force production. Somebody who starts stronger gains
more absolute Newtons from the same volume growth than somebody who starts
weaker.

Both are biologically plausible — the truth is probably somewhere between them.
But they have radically different implications for what a correlation study
shows, depending on which scale the researcher chooses.

### Why the scale of measurement changes everything

Researchers routinely express both hypertrophy and strength gains as *percentage
changes* from baseline — partly for comparability. But in a multiplicative world,
percentage changes on both sides correlate strongly with each other even if the
absolute relationship is noisy. In an additive world, using percentage changes
introduces a spurious association driven by baseline values: someone who starts
small gains a large percentage from a small absolute gain, while a large person's
identical absolute gain registers as a small percentage. The correlation you
observe is partly about the biology, and partly about where your participants
happened to start.

::: {.callout-note}
## The Marques et al. r = 0.92 through this lens

The within-person r = 0.92 uses percentage changes as the outcome. In a
multiplicative world with within-person comparison (which is *guaranteed* — each
person is compared to themselves), baseline-size noise that would inflate the
percentage-change correlation between subjects is already perfectly controlled.
The high r is more informative about the **data structure** than about the causal
magnitude.
:::

---

### 🔬 Simulation 2 — Does the Scale Lie?

200 participants with varying baseline muscle size and strength. Toggle between
an additive and multiplicative world, then compare the correlations on absolute
vs. percentage-change scales. Slide *baseline variability* up to see the
discrepancy grow.

```{shinylive-r}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 620

library(shiny)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

ui <- fluidPage(
  tags$style(HTML("
    body { font-family: 'Helvetica Neue', sans-serif; font-size:13px; }
    .well { background:#f8f8f8; border:1px solid #ddd; padding:12px; }
    h4 { font-size:13px; font-weight:700; text-transform:uppercase;
         letter-spacing:0.08em; color:#2c6e8a; margin-bottom:10px; }
    .stat-label { font-size:11px; color:#777; text-transform:uppercase; letter-spacing:.1em; }
    .stat-value { font-size:1.3rem; font-weight:700; }
  ")),
  fluidRow(
    column(4,
      wellPanel(
        h4("Data-generating world"),
        radioButtons("dgp", NULL,
          choices=c("Additive"="additive","Multiplicative"="multiplicative"),
          selected="additive"),
        hr(),
        h4("Parameters"),
        sliderInput("beta",    "True causal effect (β)",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.5, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("baseVar", "Baseline variability",
                    min=0.1, max=1, value=0.5, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("noise2",  "Noise",
                    min=0.05, max=1, value=0.3, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("n2",      "N",
                    min=50, max=500, value=200, step=50),
        actionButton("resample2","Resample",class="btn btn-primary btn-sm")
      )
    ),
    column(8,
      plotOutput("two_plots", height="340px"),
      hr(),
      fluidRow(
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","r (absolute)"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#2c6e8a",textOutput("r_abs",inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","r (percentage)"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#2d7d46",textOutput("r_pct",inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","|r_abs − r_pct|"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#c0392b",textOutput("r_gap",inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","True β"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#555",textOutput("beta_out",inline=TRUE))
        )
      ),
      p(style="font-size:12px;color:#777;margin-top:10px;",
        "The gap between the two correlations is entirely an artefact of scale choice — ",
        "not a change in the underlying biology.")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {

  sim2 <- reactive({
    input$resample2
    n   <- input$n2
    b   <- input$beta
    bv  <- input$baseVar
    sig <- input$noise2

    S0  <- pmax(0.2, 1 + bv * rnorm(n))
    F0  <- pmax(0.2, 1 + bv * rnorm(n))

    dS_abs <- pmax(0.01, 0.1 + 0.05 * rnorm(n) + sig * 0.03 * rnorm(n))

    if (input$dgp == "additive") {
      dF_abs <- b * dS_abs + sig * 0.08 * rnorm(n)
    } else {
      pct_s  <- dS_abs / S0
      pct_f  <- b * pct_s + sig * 0.05 * rnorm(n)
      dF_abs <- pct_f * F0
    }

    data.frame(
      dS_abs = dS_abs,
      dF_abs = dF_abs,
      pct_dS = dS_abs / S0,
      pct_dF = dF_abs / pmax(0.01, F0)
    )
  })

  output$two_plots <- renderPlot({
    d <- sim2()
    r_abs <- cor(d$dS_abs, d$dF_abs)
    r_pct <- cor(d$pct_dS, d$pct_dF)

    p1 <- ggplot(d, aes(dS_abs, dF_abs)) +
      geom_point(colour="#2c6e8a", alpha=0.35, size=1.8) +
      geom_smooth(method="lm", se=TRUE, colour="#c0392b",
                  fill="#e74c3c", alpha=0.12, linetype="dashed", linewidth=0.8) +
      labs(x="Δ Size (absolute)", y="Δ Strength (absolute)",
           title=sprintf("Absolute  —  r = %.2f", r_abs)) +
      theme_minimal(base_size=12) +
      theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold", colour="#2c6e8a"))

    p2 <- ggplot(d, aes(pct_dS, pct_dF)) +
      geom_point(colour="#2d7d46", alpha=0.35, size=1.8) +
      geom_smooth(method="lm", se=TRUE, colour="#c0392b",
                  fill="#e74c3c", alpha=0.12, linetype="dashed", linewidth=0.8) +
      labs(x="% Δ Size", y="% Δ Strength",
           title=sprintf("Percentage  —  r = %.2f", r_pct)) +
      theme_minimal(base_size=12) +
      theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold", colour="#2d7d46"))

    p1 + p2
  })

  output$r_abs  <- renderText({ d<-sim2(); sprintf("%.2f", cor(d$dS_abs,d$dF_abs)) })
  output$r_pct  <- renderText({ d<-sim2(); sprintf("%.2f", cor(d$pct_dS,d$pct_dF)) })
  output$r_gap  <- renderText({
    d<-sim2()
    sprintf("%.2f", abs(cor(d$dS_abs,d$dF_abs) - cor(d$pct_dS,d$pct_dF)))
  })
  output$beta_out <- renderText({ sprintf("%.2f", input$beta) })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

> In the **additive world**, push baseline variability to 0.8 and watch the
> percentage-change correlation diverge from the absolute one — the two are
> measuring different things. In the **multiplicative world**, the roles reverse.
> The biology did not change; only the analyst's choice of scale did.

---

## Part III — Putting It Together: The Identification Problem

Even if we know the true causal structure *and* the correct scale, a further
problem remains: **a wide range of true causal worlds can produce the same
observed correlation.** The simulation below lets you adjust all parameters
simultaneously. The grey band marks r ∈ [0.85, 0.95] — roughly what Marques
et al. observed. See how many distinct causal configurations land inside it.

```{shinylive-r}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 600

library(shiny)
library(ggplot2)

ui <- fluidPage(
  tags$style(HTML("
    body { font-family:'Helvetica Neue',sans-serif; font-size:13px; }
    .well { background:#f8f8f8; border:1px solid #ddd; padding:12px; }
    h4 { font-size:13px; font-weight:700; text-transform:uppercase;
         letter-spacing:.08em; color:#2c6e8a; margin-bottom:10px; }
    .stat-label { font-size:11px; color:#777; text-transform:uppercase; letter-spacing:.1em; }
    .stat-value { font-size:1.3rem; font-weight:700; }
    #target_msg { font-weight:700; font-size:1rem; padding:6px 12px; border-radius:3px; }
  ")),
  fluidRow(
    column(4,
      wellPanel(
        h4("Causal structure"),
        sliderInput("s3_direct", "Direct effect: Size → Strength",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.4, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("s3_common", "Common cause: Training → both",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.6, step=0.05),
        h4("Measurement"),
        sliderInput("s3_scale",  "Scale (0 = absolute, 1 = % change)",
                    min=0, max=1, value=1.0, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("s3_bv",     "Baseline variability",
                    min=0.1, max=1, value=0.5, step=0.05),
        h4("Noise & sample"),
        sliderInput("s3_noise",  "Noise",
                    min=0.05, max=0.8, value=0.25, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("s3_n",      "N",
                    min=50, max=500, value=200, step=50),
        actionButton("resample3","Resample",class="btn btn-primary btn-sm")
      )
    ),
    column(8,
      plotOutput("scatter3", height="320px"),
      hr(),
      fluidRow(
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","Observed r"),
          div(class="stat-value", textOutput("r3_obs", inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","Causal fraction"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#2d7d46", textOutput("r3_causal", inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","Confound fraction"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#c0392b", textOutput("r3_confound", inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          uiOutput("target_msg")
        )
      ),
      p(style="font-size:12px;color:#777;margin-top:10px;",
        "Grey band = target r ∈ [0.85, 0.95] (approx. Marques et al. 2025). ",
        "How many different causal worlds hit the same target?")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {

  sim3 <- reactive({
    input$resample3
    n    <- input$s3_n
    b_d  <- input$s3_direct
    b_c  <- input$s3_common
    sig  <- input$s3_noise
    sc   <- input$s3_scale
    bv   <- input$s3_bv

    training <- rnorm(n)
    S0 <- pmax(0.2, 1 + bv * rnorm(n))
    F0 <- pmax(0.2, 1 + bv * rnorm(n))

    dS_abs <- pmax(0.01, b_c * training * 0.15 + 0.08 + sig * 0.05 * rnorm(n))
    dF_direct  <- b_d * dS_abs
    dF_common  <- b_c * training * 0.15
    dF_abs <- dF_direct + dF_common + sig * 0.05 * rnorm(n)

    # blend between absolute and % scale
    x_mix <- (1 - sc) * dS_abs + sc * (dS_abs / S0)
    y_mix <- (1 - sc) * dF_abs + sc * (dF_abs / pmax(0.01, F0))

    list(
      x = x_mix, y = y_mix,
      causal_frac  = b_d^2 / (b_d^2 + b_c^2 + sig^2 + 1e-4),
      confound_frac = b_c^2 / (b_d^2 + b_c^2 + sig^2 + 1e-4)
    )
  })

  output$scatter3 <- renderPlot({
    s   <- sim3()
    r   <- cor(s$x, s$y)
    d   <- data.frame(x=s$x, y=s$y)

    # y-range for target band
    yr  <- range(s$y)
    band_lo <- yr[1] + 0.85 * diff(yr)
    band_hi <- yr[1] + 0.95 * diff(yr)

    ggplot(d, aes(x, y)) +
      annotate("rect",
               xmin=-Inf, xmax=Inf,
               ymin=band_lo, ymax=band_hi,
               fill="grey80", alpha=0.4) +
      annotate("text", x=min(d$x)+0.01*diff(range(d$x)),
               y=mean(c(band_lo,band_hi)),
               label="target r ∈ [0.85, 0.95]",
               hjust=0, size=3, colour="grey50") +
      geom_point(colour="#2c6e8a", alpha=0.35, size=1.8) +
      geom_smooth(method="lm", se=TRUE,
                  colour="#c0392b", fill="#e74c3c",
                  alpha=0.12, linetype="dashed", linewidth=0.8) +
      labs(x = ifelse(input$s3_scale < 0.5, "Δ Size (absolute mix)", "% Δ Size (pct mix)"),
           y = ifelse(input$s3_scale < 0.5, "Δ Strength (absolute mix)", "% Δ Strength (pct mix)"),
           title = sprintf("Observed r = %.2f", r)) +
      theme_minimal(base_size=13) +
      theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold",colour="#1a1a1a"))
  })

  output$r3_obs     <- renderText({ sprintf("%.2f", cor(sim3()$x, sim3()$y)) })
  output$r3_causal  <- renderText({ sprintf("%.0f%%", 100 * sim3()$causal_frac) })
  output$r3_confound <- renderText({ sprintf("%.0f%%", 100 * sim3()$confound_frac) })

  output$target_msg <- renderUI({
    r <- cor(sim3()$x, sim3()$y)
    in_target <- r >= 0.85 & r <= 0.95
    div(id="target_msg",
        style=paste0("background:", if(in_target) "#d5f5e3" else "#fdecea",
                     ";color:", if(in_target) "#1e8449" else "#922b21", ";"),
        if(in_target) "✓ In target zone" else "✗ Outside target"
    )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

> You should find it easy to achieve r > 0.85 even with the direct effect at
> zero, by increasing the common-cause coefficient and switching to percentage
> scaling. Conversely, a substantial direct effect can yield r ≈ 0.3 if baseline
> variability is high and you measure absolute changes in a multiplicative world.
> **The same number can arise from very different data-generating processes.**

---

## What Would Actually Help?

To answer whether muscle size *causally* affects strength, we need one of the
following:

**1. Experimental variation in size independent of training load.** If you could
randomly assign participants to gain different amounts of muscle (via BFR, very
different rep ranges equated for effort, or pharmacological aids) while holding
training volume and motor learning constant, you could estimate the causal
effect. Some designs come close, but unmeasured confounders remain.

**2. A formal causal mediation analysis** — which attempts to decompose the
total effect of training into the portion that runs *through* muscle growth and
the portion that bypasses it (neural adaptations, etc.).

Crucially, however, even causal mediation analysis makes strong assumptions that
are unlikely to hold perfectly. **Part II of this series** will dig into the
mediation framework, and **Part III** will show exactly where those assumptions
break — and what happens to our estimates when they do.

---

## Summary

| Problem | Mechanism | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Confounding | Training drives both size and strength | Randomise size *independently* of training |
| Collider bias | Selecting on a common effect of both | Avoid conditioning on downstream variables |
| Scale artefact | Absolute vs. % change in add./mult. world | Pre-register scale; sensitivity across both |
| Identification | Many causal worlds → same r | Need causal assumptions, not just data |

Correlations between hypertrophy and strength gains arise from at least three
structural mechanisms, any of which can produce arbitrarily high observed
correlations. No correlation statistic — Pearson, repeated-measures, or
otherwise — can distinguish between them without additional assumptions about
the causal graph.

Beyond causal structure, the scale of measurement interacts with the
data-generating process to produce correlations that reflect the researcher's
analytic choices as much as the underlying biology.

The conclusion is not that muscle size does not cause strength — it clearly
contributes. The conclusion is that the correlation coefficient is a blunt,
causally agnostic tool that cannot tell us how much it contributes, under what
conditions, or for whom. For that, we need a different framework.

*That is where we are going next.*

---

*Simulations are stochastic; resample to build intuition across runs. All data
are synthetic.*
